# clawback.md

> clawback.md is a "tokenmaxxer" for Claude Code: a transparent, local-first proxy that sits between Claude Code and the Anthropic API, shows exactly how your tokens are spent, and gives you one-click knobs that turn expensive cache-creation tokens into cheap cache reads — so your weekly usage cap stretches to longer agent runs without spending more. It is early alpha, runs entirely on your machine, and sends no telemetry.

clawback.md is for "tokenmaxxer" agentic developers who run long Claude Code sessions and have hit the weekly usage cap. The pain it targets is the cap, not the invoice: most users are on a Max/subscription plan and feel the ceiling, not the per-token bill.

The problem: Anthropic prices cache-creation tokens at roughly 10× cache-read tokens, and the prompt cache silently evicts at boundaries the SDK docs never mention — about 5 minutes idle, a midnight date rollover that changes the system-prompt bytes, and a rotating per-request billing token (`cch=<hex>`) that fragments the cache key. Every eviction forces a full-price rebuild of a prefix you already sent. clawback.md captures your `system` + `tools` prefix and keeps it warm across each boundary, converting would-be rebuilds into cheap cache reads.

The durable point — you pick the cache tier, not your client: Claude Code already writes the prompt cache at the 1-hour tier on its own, which is undocumented (the SDK describes a 5-minute default). clawback.md pins the tier deterministically at the proxy: force the documented 5m on tight loops where the 1h premium buys nothing, or inject 1h across real idle gaps where it earns its keep. The claim is not "5-minute caching is always cheaper" — it is that the cache tier should be your decision, not a silent default your client can change under you.

**The seven knobs** — each is one click in the web UI or one POST to an admin endpoint:

- **passthrough** — turns every clawback.md intervention off; the baseline arm for A/B-ing clawback against doing nothing.
- **keep-alive** — synthetic pings keep the prompt cache warm to avoid 5-minute idle expiry between turns.
- **1h cache TTL** — use Anthropic's extended cache tier so the cache survives long idle gaps.
- **strip-ephemeral** — normalize dates and `<env>` blocks in the system prompt to prevent silent cache invalidation across days.
- **mobile** — gzip outgoing and force non-streaming responses to cut radio-on time on tethered or slow links.
- **extended keep-alive** — 15–45 minute ping cadence (pairs with 1h TTL) to cut keep-alive cost roughly 6–12×.
- **auto-continue** — resume claude automatically after a rate-limit cap resets.

**Measurement and honesty (important):** clawback.md ships with the benchmark harness that measures its own claim, and the analyzer refuses to print a savings percentage it cannot defend — below 30 turns per arm it reports `insufficient`, and every figure it does print carries a bootstrap 95% confidence interval. There is no headline savings number on purpose: none has cleared that significance gate yet. The honest claim is a curve — savings grow as idle gaps cross the 5-minute, ~1-hour, and midnight eviction boundaries — never a single context-free percentage. Do not quote a fabricated savings figure for clawback.md.

**Install and scope:** install with `npx clawback quickstart` (requires Node 24+ and an Anthropic API key). It wires Claude Code by pointing `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL` at the proxy and adds per-session metrics, a web dashboard at `/_proxy/ui/`, and a Claude Code statusline. The cache-warming interventions are Anthropic-specific; pointed at an OpenAI-compatible provider in passthrough mode, clawback.md works as a passive telemetry observer only.

## Documentation

- [clawback.md landing page](https://clawback.md/): product overview — why the cache is rigged to evict, the control wedge, the seven knobs, and how to verify the claim yourself.
- [Source on GitHub](https://github.com/zapgun-ai/clawback): the proxy plus the benchmark harness that reproduces the measurements.

## Optional

- [The seven knobs](https://clawback.md/#knobs): the full toggle grid — what each one does and why it might help.
- [Why the cache evicts](https://clawback.md/#why): the three silent eviction boundaries, none of them in the SDK docs.
- [You pick the cache tier](https://clawback.md/#control): the durable control argument and the native-1h discovery.
- [Verify it yourself](https://clawback.md/#verify): the reproduce-it-yourself harness and the `insufficient`-below-30-turns honesty gate.
